coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of different covalent crosslinking agents into valsartan-loaded sericin and alginate particles for modified release
coredata.dc:creator: Santinon, Caroline
Borges, Daniele
da Silva, Meuris Gurgel Carlos
Vieira, Melissa Gurgel Adeodato
coredata.dc:description: Valsartan is a drug indicated for hypertension and heart failure treatment, being administered for many years by the patient. Its release modification by incorporation into a polymeric blend, such as sericin and alginate, brings benefits as the marked decrease in side effects and the possibility of decreasing the dose administration frequency. Four covalent crosslinking agents were tested in the incorporation stage, in order to optimize the formulation. Proanthocyanidin was the most promising crosslinker and its concentration was varied to assess the influence on drug diffusion. A 2% (w/V) content promoted 40% of incorporation efficiency increase. The in vitro dissolution exhibited a prolonged profile and the drug loading was closer to the theoretical calculated than the formulation without crosslinking agent. Proanthocyanidin promoted the non-interaction of the particles with the gelatinous capsule and ensured the stability of valsartan. Particles characterizations confirmed the effective drug incorporation and a thermal behavior improvement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sericin
Alginate
Modified drug release
Crosslinking agents
Proanthocyanidin

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Council for Scientific and Technological Development, CNPq (Grant number 301401/2016-0), The São Paulo Research Foundation, FAPESP (Grant numbers 2015/13505-9, 2016/05007-1) and Coordination for the Improvement of Higher Education Personnel, CAPES (Grant number 88882.329675/2019-01). The authors would like to thank the BRATAC Silk Mills Company for providing the silkworm cocoons.

This work was supported by the National Council for Scientific and Technological Development , CNPq (Grant number 301401/2016-0 ), The São Paulo Research Foundation , FAPESP (Grant numbers 2015/13505-9 , 2016/05007-1 ) and Coordination for the Improvement of Higher Education Personnel , CAPES (Grant number 88882.329675/2019-01 ). The authors would like to thank the BRATAC Silk Mills Company for providing the silkworm cocoons.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Incorporation efficiency for formulations with crosslinking agents compared to the initial formulations (a) VAL-1 and (b) VAL-2.
Drug loading and theoretical loading for formulations produced with crosslinking agents for (a) VAL-1 and (b) VAL-2.
Valsartan release profiles in particles containing crosslinking agents for (a) VAL-1 and (b) VAL-2, compared to the formulation without the agents.
Size distribution profile for formulations with 2% (w/V) of crosslinking agents, compared to particles drug-free (SAAC2, SAPRO2, SAPEG2 and SAPVA2).
Size distribution profile for formulations with varying concentration of crosslinking agents, citric acid and proanthocyanidin, compared to particles without drug.
Size distribution profile for the formulations varying the concentration of proanthocyanidin in the initial formulation VAL-2, compared to drug-free formulations.

Micrographs obtained from the formulations with different crosslinking agents in 2% (w/V) with magnifications of (a) 150× of the outer particle surface and (b) 1500× from the cross-section of the particle. The magnification micrograph of (b) 1500× was also performed for the pure drug (VAL).

Micrographs obtained from the formulations with crosslinking agents in varying concentrations. The figures have magnifications of (a) 150× of the outer particle surface and (b) 1500× after the particle cross-section for the VAL-1 and (a) 150× magnifications of the outer particle surface and (c) 1000× after the particle cross section for VAL-2.
TGA / DTA and DTG profiles obtained for formulations with the different types of covalent crosslinking agents added.
HSM micrographs for the most promising formulations (VPRO2 and V2PRO2).
FTIR spectrum of formulations with different crosslinking agents and the best formulations (VPRO2 and V2PRO2) compared to formulations without drug (SAPRO2 and SA2PRO2).
X-ray diffractograms of pure valsartan and particles with incorporated drug considered more favorable, compared to the formulations without drug.
Release profile of the (a) VPRO2 and (b) V2PRO2 formulations after accelerated stability study, in comparison with the curve obtained by the initial condition.
Proposed formulations and their respective compositions.
Difference and similarity factors for the dissolution profiles of the particles with the crosslinking agents in comparison with the reference formulations VAL-1 and VAL-2, respectively.
Release of incorporated valsartan in acidic medium.
Parameters obtained from the release of VAL-1 formulations without and with the crosslinkers in different proportions.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The numbers highlighted in bold indicate a better fit of the model.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Incorporation efficiency and drug loading after 3 and 6 months of the accelerated stability test.
Difference and similarity factors for the dissolution profiles of formulations tested for stability.

head.ce:title: Evaluation of different covalent crosslinking agents into valsartan-loaded sericin and alginate particles for modified release

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Processes and Products Design, School of Chemical Engineering, University of Campinas – UNICAMP, Albert Einstein Av., 500, 13083-852 Campinas, SP, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Processes and Products Design

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Processes and Products Design, School of Chemical Engineering, University of Campinas – UNICAMP, Albert Einstein Av., 500, 13083-852 Campinas, SP, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Valsartan is a drug indicated for hypertension and heart failure treatment, being administered for many years by the patient. Its release modification by incorporation into a polymeric blend, such as sericin and alginate, brings benefits as the marked decrease in side effects and the possibility of decreasing the dose administration frequency. Four covalent crosslinking agents were tested in the incorporation stage, in order to optimize the formulation. Proanthocyanidin was the most promising crosslinker and its concentration was varied to assess the influence on drug diffusion. A 2% (w/V) content promoted 40% of incorporation efficiency increase. The in vitro dissolution exhibited a prolonged profile and the drug loading was closer to the theoretical calculated than the formulation without crosslinking agent. Proanthocyanidin promoted the non-interaction of the particles with the gelatinous capsule and ensured the stability of valsartan. Particles characterizations confirmed the effective drug incorporation and a thermal behavior improvement.

ce:section.ce:para: Valsartan is a drug usually indicated for hypertension treatment, categorized as an angiotensin II receptor (vasoconstrictor) blocker which causes vasodilation of the arteries and consequently regulates blood pressure [1]. Its conventional form of release shows peak plasma concentration in 2 to 4 h after the administration, being not suitable for the correct treatment of this disease [2].

Although many drugs already exist in the extended pharmaceutical form, valsartan is not yet commercialized, being only found in the immediate release form. There are two patents available for modifying the form of release of valsartan, one of which has already been approved by the FDA - Food and Drug Administration filed by the company Ezra Innovations (US 9,687,475 B1) [3]. The other patent was filed by Novartis (US 2010/0233253 A1) [4], holder of the reference drug for valsartan, Diovan®. None of these in-process patents are aimed at the production of particles, but rather tablets. The release modification of this drug through its incorporation into a polymeric matrix, as a multiparticulate system form, brings benefits such as the marked reduction in side effects present in conventional release. In addition, the dose administration frequency may decrease, as the API (Ingredient Active Pharmacist) will act for a prolonged period in the organism [5,6].

Sericin is a biopolymer extracted from the silkworm cocoon and can be applied in biomedical and pharmaceutical areas due to its relevant characteristics such as biodegradability, antioxidative and anticoagulant properties, in addition to being easily associated with other polymers, which can be attributed to its side polar groups [7–9]. This material comes from one of the stages of the silk production process because the removal of sericin is responsible for ensuring shine and softness to the fabric. This disposal can be discarded into wastewater, which might negatively affect the environment [10].

Sericin extracted by hot water, despite having advantages over other extraction methods [11], presents fragility in terms of pH and temperatures, in addition to forming low-resistant particles, requiring the addition of another biopolymer in order to form a blend [12,13]. Therefore, the alginates family derived from brown algae (Phaeophyceae) may be added at the stage of particle formation due to their gelation capacity when in contact with multivalent cations, such as Ca2+. Alginates are already widely applied in several areas, including food and pharmaceuticals, evidencing its non-toxicity [14–17].

The addition of chemical crosslinking agents in the formulation is responsible for creating interconnections between the molecules, increasing the obstacles to the diffusion of the drug by the approximation of the macromolecular chains, which reduces the effective area available for the release of the API through the polymeric matrix [18]. This feature can favor the increase of the drug incorporation efficiency in the blend and the prolongation release profile by the drug because of the higher crosslinking degree achieved and the consequent drug entrapment advance through the three-dimensional network formed [19,20].

The use of crosslinking agents in a sericin and alginate matrix has already been successfully applied for optimization of drug formulations such as ibuprofen by using polyethylene glycol (PEG) [21] and proanthocyanidin for both ketoprofen [22] and furosemide [23]. Along with these, the crosslinking agents poly vinylalcohol (PVA), polyethylene glycol (PEG) and disodium phosphate (DSP) were also evaluated for the incorporation of naproxen in the same polymeric matrix [20]. No type of covalent crosslinking agent has been previously tested for valsartan drug in this same polymeric matrix of sericin and alginate in order to modify the release.

Therefore, the citric acid, PVA, PEG and proanthocyanidin covalent crosslinking agents were explored in this paper to incorporate the valsartan drug also in a sericin and alginate matrix. Citric acid was tested due to its multicarboxylic structure, being able to promote crosslinking interaction between its carboxylic groups and the hydroxyl groups of the polymers [24].

Two main formulations (VAL-1 and VAL-2), that were previously explored by Santinon et al. [25] along with other formulations, were chosen because they presented the least favorable results for incorporation efficiency and drug loading with no crosslinkers added, yet with the most prolonged in vitro release profiles among the 5 formulations tested. In this way, the crosslinking agents tested in this paper were able, in general, to improve these results and maintain a prolonged release profile of valsartan. All formulations produced were characterized by OM (Optical Microscopy), SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy) and DTA/TGA (Thermogravimetry and Differential Thermal Analysis). Those who obtained the most promising result, i.e., the ones that showed the best results regarding the incorporation efficiency, drug loading by the particles and in vitro release at acidic pH (1.2) and simulated enteric medium (6.8), concomitantly, with the respective crosslinking agent were characterized by FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy), XRD (X-ray diffraction) and HSM (Hot-Stage Microscopy) analytical techniques. Finally, accelerated stability study was also conducted for the most promising formulations.

Silkworm cocoons were supplied by the BRATAC company (Parana, Brazil). Valsartan (PhitoFormulas, SP, Brazil), sodium alginate (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), anhydrous calcium chloride powder (Anidrol™, SP, Brazil), sodium hydroxide (Dinâmica, SP, Brazil), monobasic potassium phosphate (Dinâmica, SP, Brazil) and deionized water (Reverse osmosis, Gehaka, Brazil) were used in this research. As crosslinking agents, proanthocyanidin (Galena, SP, Brazil), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), polyethylene glycol (PEG) (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and citric acid (Dinâmica, SP, Brazil) were used.

Following the method proposed by Silva et al. [26], the pieces of cocoons previously cleaned and cut were washed and dried in an oven (approximately at 40 °C for 24 h). Then, 80 g of these cocoons were added in 2 l of deionized water and posteriorly placed in a vertical autoclave (AV-18, Phoenix, Brazil) during 40 min aiming for the sericin extraction. After separating the remaining fibroin through filtration, sericin was stored in a closed container at room temperature until complete hydrogel stabilization, and then freeze. To produce the particles, the freezing and thawing technique were applied and sericin of higher molar mass was used as the base solution, after its concentration adjusted to 2.5% (w/V), according with Da Silva and co-authors [27].

The adjusted sericin solution was resolubilized in autoclave and, after reaching a temperature of 55 °C, sodium alginate (2% and 2.8% w/V, respectively), valsartan (2% w/V) and the covalent crosslinking agents varying the concentrations were added into different solutions (Table 1 ). The mixture was then stirred with the UltraTurrax® (T18, IKA, USA) equipment (4000 to 8000 rpm) for 30 min. The particles were produced with the aid of peristaltic pumps and dropping tubes (Tygon, L/S 14), slowly dripping the solution prepared in a solution of calcium chloride (3% w/V) and posteriorly placed in jar test (JT-203, Milan, Brazil) at 100 rpm during 30 min in order to guarantee the complete formation of particles. After this step, the particles were washed thoroughly and dried at room temperature [21,28,29].

According to Khandai et al. [28] and Vidart et al. [29] methodologies, 0.1 g of dry particles were placed in contact with 500 mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.8) during 24 h. After this period, the solutions were sonicated (LS-9.5DA, LimpSonic, Brazil) for 15 min and the samples were filtered (0.45 μm). The drug content present was determined by UV–Vis (UV-1900, Shimadzu, Japan) at 250 nm wavelength. The percentages of IE (incorporation efficiency) and DL (drug loading) of each formulation were calculated according to Eqs. 1, 2 and 3 [30].
(1)
% IE =
Experimental drug content Theoretical drug content
x
100
(2)
% DL =
Weight of drug in the particles
Weight of particles
x
100
(3)
Theoretical
% DL =
Weigh of
d rug
inicially
added
Weight

particles
x
100

Drug release test was conducted based on the recommendation dictated by U.S. Pharmacopeia 42-NF37 [31] by adding an amount of particles equal to 160 mg in the USP apparatus 1 (Logan, UDT-814 model, USA) along with 1000 mL of phosphate buffer medium (pH 6.8) at 50 rpm and 37 ± 0.5 °C temperature. Aliquots were collected at determined times and concentrations read in UV–Vis equipment (250 nm). The formulations that showed the most promising results were also tested at pH 1.2 for 2 h, in order to evaluate the gastro-resistant character of the particles.

To assess the similarity of the two dissolution curves obtained, Eqs. 4 and 5, representing the difference (f
1 ) and similarity (f
2 ) factors were applied [32,33].
(4)
f 1 =
∑
t = 1
n
Rt − Tt
/
∑
t = 1
n
Rt
x
100
(5)
f 2 = 50
log
1 +
1 n
∑
t = 1
n
Rt − Tt
2
− 0.5
x
100

In which t corresponds to the number of samples, n expresses the number of collection times, Rt and Tt represents the dissolved percentage of the reference formulation and the product tested, respectively. This method directly compares the drug content dissolved over time between two different dissolution curves.

The kinetic behavior of valsartan drug through these polymeric matrices was evaluated by fitting six different mathematical models expressed by Eqs. 6 to 11 for the experimental data, with the aid of the Maple 17™ software. The calculation of the parameters R2 adj and AIC - Akaike Information Criterion helped to determine the best fit obtained, with lower AIC values indicating a better fit [34], following the methodology used by Dash et al. [35] and Freitas et al. [21,36,37].

Zero order reaction:
(6)
Qt =
Q 0

K 0

First order reaction:
(7)

Higuchi's model:
(8)

K H

n
where, Qt expresses the amount of drug dissolved over time (t), usually pointed as hours. Q
0 indicates the initial amount of drug in solution and K
0
, K
1
, K
H
e K
KP corresponds to zero order, first order, Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas release constant, respectively [5,38,39].

In the empirical equation described by Weibull [40] and later adapted by Langenbucher [41], described by Eq. 9, T represents the delay time until dissolution occurs and in most cases its value is zero; a is a scale parameter corresponding to the process time and b characterizes the dissolution curve in exponential (b = 1), sigmoid (b > 1) or parabolic (b < 1).

In Korsmeyer-Peppas and Hopfenberg model (Eqs. 10 and 11),
Q
Q ∞
represents the fractional release of the drug over time t. The difference is found in parameter n, where in the Korsmeyer-Peppas model indicates the exponent associated with the release mechanism (values between 0.5 and 1) and in the Hopfenberg model, however, n considers the geometry of the system in plates (n = 1), cylinders (n = 2) and spheres (n = 3). Also, in this last model, C
0 expresses the initial drug concentration present in the matrix and a
0 is the initial radius of the sphere, cylinder or plate [42,43].

The dry particles produced from the different formulations with crosslinking agents were characterized by the techniques: a) Optical Microscope (National Stereo Microscope DC4-456H) to calculate the average diameter of 500 particles and posteriorly distribute them to the Gauss adjustment; b) Scanning Electron Microscope (LEO; LEO 44i) in which the particles were covered with a thin layer of gold; c) Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific; Nicolet 6700) where the samples were analyzed as KBr tablets in the 400–4000 cm−1 range; d) X-ray diffraction (Philips Analytical X Ray; X'Pert-MPD) with Cu-Kα radiation; 40 kV, 40 mA. 5–50° incidence angle and 0.02 ° step; e) Thermogravimetry and Differential Thermal Analysis (Shimadzu; DTG-60) using N2 atmosphere, 50 mL / min flow rate, 20 °C / min heating rate and 30 to 1000 °C temperature; f) Differential Scanning Calorimetry (Mettler-Toledo; DSC1) with N2 atmosphere, 50 mL / min flow rate, 10 °C / min heating rate of room temperature to 400 °C range; g) Hot Stage Microscopy (Mettler-Toledo; FP84HT; HOT STAGE FP82HT; FP90 Central Processor) specified as 25 to 250 °C temperature range at 10 °C / min rate.

Analyzing Fig. 1a, 2% (w/V) of citric acid did not seem to favor the drug incorporation by the blend, which may indicate that some raw material may have been lost during the particle production process, justifying the outlier obtained. Although citric acid favors the interaction between polymers for its performance at the interface/surface tension of polymeric films [44], this agent provided particles with more fragile characteristics and unfavorable ion exchange with the calcium chloride solution, which led to the particles agglutination in the drying step. One possibility for this effect comes from the fact that citric acid has been reported together with sodium bicarbonate for floating polymer systems using valsartan, which radically reduces the density of the pharmaceutical form so that it remains floating in the stomach for a longer period [45,46]. Therefore, the formulation containing 2% (w/V) of citric acid was discarded from the next steps, since the production of the particles became unviable. Nevertheless, the incorporation of this crosslinking agent in a lower concentration (0.5% w/V) was tested in order to assess whether the viscosity would be less affected.

When comparing the results obtained by formulations containing 2% (w/V) of crosslinking agents (Fig. 1a), it was found that PVA provided an increase of around 21%, while the results obtained by formulations containing proanthocyanidin and PEG were more promising, showing an increase of approximately 40.5% and 58.5%, respectively, in relation to the formulation without any crosslinking agents (VAL-1).

The IE of valsartan with PEG slightly exceeded the limit of 100%, but still showing acceptable values within the calculated standard deviation, not eliminating the viability of this formulation. This event may be associated with loss of raw material, in the same way as citric acid (VAC2).

As demonstrated by more promising results, along with other aspects as its biological activity and good applicability in drugs uses [47], proanthocyanidin was tested in other concentrations (0.5, 1 and 3% w/V), at the initial formulation VAL-1, in order to evaluate the influence of this crosslinking agent on the particles production, also presented by Fig. 1a. This same agent was incorporated in concentrations of 1% and 2% (w/V) at the initial formulation VAL-2, which contained a higher content of sodium alginate (2.8% w/V), and are demonstrated in Fig. 1b. The concentration of 3% (w/V) was not evaluated because the high solution viscosity would impair the dripping step.

Fig. 1a shows that the formulation VAC0.5 presented more promising results for the IE of valsartan, when compared to VAC2 formulation, with an increase of 43% for this result. However, the fragile characteristic of the particles continued to be present in this formulation, but with less intensity when compared to VAC2.

Proanthocyanidin inserted in proportions of 0.5 and 1% (w/V) were not sufficient to promote a greater degree of crosslinking between the components of the formulation, as seen in Fig. 1a, the results of IE were similar with 95% confidence (Tukey Test) to formulation without any crosslinking agent (VAL-1). When added in a concentration of 3% (w/V), proanthocyanidin allowed a significant increase in relation to the initial formulation, but statistically equal to 95% confidence to the VPRO2 formulation, which contained 2% (w/V) of this same agent. As for the original formulation VAL-2 (Fig. 1b), both concentrations of 1 and 2% (w/V) promoted considerable and statistically different increases in the IE results.

DL was also evaluated and according to Fig. 2a, citric acid (VAC2 and VAC0.5) was the crosslinking agent that most influenced the drug loading by the particles. The formulation containing PEG (VPEG2) also stood out and presented a statistically different result from the others. VPRO2 and VPRO3 also achieved satisfactory results for being close to the calculated theoretical value.

In Fig. 2b, both V2PRO1 and V2PRO2 formulations expressed DL similar to 95% confidence (Tukey test), in relation to the VAL-2 formulation, despite having presented results closer to the theoretical DL calculated.

By observing Fig. 3a, only the VPRO2 and VPVA2 formulations showed a prolonged release when compared to the reference (VAL-1). In contrast, VAC0.5 demonstrated a very rapid release, reaching 100% in about 180 min of dissolution, which indicates an unfavorable behavior despite the conducive results of IE and DL. VPA2 released only 75% of the drug throughout the dissolution period, which means that the crosslinking in this case occurred in excess between the components of the blend, not allowing a good incorporation of the drug in the blend and not being capable of an effective release.

As for Fig. 3b, both formulations (V2PRO1 and V2PRO2) achieved adequate release values compared to the reference formulation VAL-2. However, while the formulation V2PRO2 reached a release value of approximately 90% of valsartan in the simulated medium, the formulation V2PRO1 stabilized its release of drug around 78%, after 1200 min of assay.

For a better understanding of the release profiles shown in Fig. 3, the factors f
1 and f
2 were calculated in order to assess the similarity of the obtained dissolution curves. According to Shah et al. [32], two release profiles can be considered similar when the values of f
1 are between 0 and 15 and the f
2 factor is between 50 and 100. Therefore, Table 2 indicates the achieved values for these factors.

The t 85% parameter is a generic estimate for several drugs and was calculated to predict the time needed to release 85% of valsartan in the phosphate buffer medium (pH 6.8). To obtain this information, the k constant of the kinetic models obtained by the in vitro dissolution data were used.

For the reference formulation VAL-1, only the formulation VPRO2 obtained values of f
1 between 0 and 15 and f
2 between 50 and 100. This proves that only this formulation managed to maintain the prolonged release profile acquired by the reference formulation, with the other formulations being outside this standard. Furthermore, when comparing the t 85% of the VAL-1 formulation (360.05 min) with this same result for the VPRO2 formulation (523.2 min), it is possible to confirm that 2% (w/V) of proanthocyanidin prolonged the release of the drug.

Regarding the VAL-2 reference formulation, V2PRO1 and V2PRO2 did not present values of f
1 between 0 and 15 and f
2 between 50 and 100. However, the release profile of V2PRO1 was more similar to the profile of the formulation without the crosslinking agent. Although it was not possible to calculate the t 85% of V2PRO1, it is noteworthy that V2PRO2 (528.44 min) reached a more prolonged form of release than VAL-2 (327.88 min).

From these results, it can be deduced that the sodium alginate composition present directly interferes with the results of incorporation efficiency, drug loading and in vitro release of valsartan, which means that by increasing the proportion of sodium alginate from 2 to 2.8% (w/V), an amount of 1% (w/V) of proanthocyanidin is sufficient to promote satisfactory results.

Additionally, the gastro-resistance of the particles that showed more favorable results, regarding IE, DL and in vitro release at phosphate buffer (pH 6.8), were verified and the data obtained are presented in Table 3 .

According to the United States Pharmacopeia USP 30 [48], only formulations with less than 10% of the release of drugs in the acidic environment can be considered gastro-resistant. Therefore, both formulations presented gastro-resistant character of the particles, which can be attributed to the free carboxylic groups present in the sodium alginate molecules [49].

The six mathematical models displayed on item 2.6 were applied to the data obtained by the in vitro dissolution (Fig. 3). Both R2 adj and AIC parameters were analyzed and and the results are presented in Table 4 .

Overall, the Weibull model fitted well almost every formulation. From this model, the Td parameter could be estimated by (a = (Td)b) [50], confirming that the formulations VPRO2 and VPA2 took longer to release 63.2% of the drug when compared to VAL-1, in the same way that the V2PRO2 formulation took longer compared to VAL-2, as highlighted in bold for better viewing. Also, this model relates the parameter b calculated with the characterization of the curve. The formulations that presented b > 1 indicates, according to Papadopoulou et al. [51], S-shaped curve and complex mechanism involving erosion, diffusion and swelling of the polymeric matrix stages.

The first order model was also suitable for the formulations, meaning that the drug is released proportionally to the amount remaining inside the particle. In addition, the Korsmeyer-Peppas model demonstrated, except for the formulations VPVA2 and VPRO2, n > 0.89, indicating a Super Case-II transport, which means that, in these cases, the swelling and relaxation of the polymer chains are responsible for the drug release in the medium.

The zero order model showed a less satisfactory adjustment in most cases. This model predicts a constant release per unit of time, differently from what is expected for formulations with prolonged release, in which the drug must remain stable for a longer time in the patient's body. Higuchi's model was also not well adapted to some formulations, suggesting that, in these cases, the release mechanism is not governed by Fick's Law, but by the swelling and erosion of the matrix.
The optical microscopy (OM) analytical technique was applied and the image processing was performed using the ImageJ® software.

Fig. 4 shows that the particle size ranged from 1.306 ± 0.333 mm (VPEG2) to 1.978 ± 0.757 mm (VAC2) for drug-loaded particles. VAC2 and VPVA2 presented the largest deviations calculated in relation to the average diameters due to the less successful particles production step, directly affecting size homogeneity. The particles homogeneity deeply influences the production process of pharmaceutical forms, as well as the drug release [22,52].

The Gaussian adjustment for these formulations was more satisfactory for the group containing proanthocyanidin. Both formulations without drug (SAPRO2) and the one with valsartan inserted (VPRO2) reached high values of R2 adj. The SAPVA2 formulation showed the best Gaussian fit among all formulations, with an R2 adj of 0.9993. However, when compared with the formulation containing the drug (VPVA2), the adjustment decreased significantly. This can be explained by the presence of air in the particles composed of sericin, alginate, valsartan and PVA, caused by the need for greater agitation, which resulted in a higher discrepancy in the particles size.

In Fig. 5 , the variation in particle sizes ranged from 1.501 ± 0.393 mm (VPRO1) to 2.302 ± 0.817 mm (VAC0.5). VAC0.5 exhibited a high average diameter value and at the standard deviation, even after reducing the concentration of the crosslinking agent. This crosslinking agent influences the viscosity of the solution with valsartan [45,46] and the ion exchange step after the dripping of the particles is impaired, resulting in particles with varying sizes.

As for the Gaussian adjustment of the relative frequency, all formulations in Fig. 5 reached high values of R2 adj, indicating good experimental reproducibility. In the present study, both the 0.5% and 2% (w/V) values of proanthocyanidin ensured high R2 adj results, which differs from the result reported by Freitas et al. [22], who demonstrated that only the lowest amount of proanthocyanidin (0.5% w/V), in the sericin and alginate blend, reached higher R2 adj values. Therefore, it can be concluded that the size distribution is not influenced by the concentration of crosslinking agent added, but by its interaction with the blend and the consequent drug entrapment.

As for Fig. 6 , diameters varied from 0.889 ± 0.545 mm (SA2PRO2) to 1.285 ± 0.272 mm (SA2PRO1), showing very close values and high R2 adj, either with 1 or with 2% (w/V) of proanthocyanidin in the formulations, corroborating with the discussion in section 3.2. This result confirms that the particle size directly affects the drug release by the in vitro assay.
All formulations produced demonstrated suitable sizes for the realization of multiparticulate systems, which is in the order of 1000 μm [52].

Analyzing Fig. 7 , both formulations containing proanthocyanidin (VPRO2) and PVA (VPVA2) exhibited good surface homogeneity and satisfactory sphericity of the particles than other formulations, which seems to contribute to the longer release profile of valsartan through the polymeric matrix. In addition, by comparing the micrographs of the particles drug loaded and drug-free, the presence of the valsartan structure (VAL) can be noted, indicating its effective incorporation.

In Fig. 8 , good homogeneity of the particle surface was observed in the three concentrations tested (0.5, 1 and 3% w/V) for VAL-1 formulations. The sphericity remained satisfactory for the proportions of 0.5 and 1% (w/V). The formulation containing 3% (w/V) had the sphericity slightly affected and the pores are more evident inside the particle, being related to the difficulty in the dripping step due to the high viscosity and the probable incorporation of air at the particle formation stage.

The formulations containing valsartan and citric acid as crosslinker, in both proportions (2% and 0.5% w/V) presented the most evident drug structure, which may have been the reason for the faster drug release than the other formulations.

For the V2PRO1 and V2PRO2 formulations, the addition of proanthocyanidin favored the sphericity of the particle, in both concentrations. The sphericity is more satisfactory under the proportion of 1% (w/V) and is related to the lower viscosity of the solution when compared to the concentration of 2% (w/V). The drug crystals appear in the drug-loaded formulations produced, indicating the effective incorporation of valsartan into the polymeric matrix with all crosslinkers tested.

To evaluate the thermal behavior of the different produced formulations, thermogravimetric (TG) and thermal differential (DTA) analyzes were performed. In all formulations (Fig. 9 ) there is a decay of the mass around 100 °C, which is usually related to the loss of water suffered by the material. DTA curves show two thermal events around 220 and 400 °C, identified as the standard behavior of materials produced with sericin. The literature reports the values of 212 °C - 319 °C, related to molecular movement and melting of peptide bonds and events of thermal decomposition for sericin [53]. The increase in these values obtained by the formulations produced ensures an improvement in thermal stability due to the crosslinking between sericin and alginate. In formulations containing proanthocyanidin, an endothermic peak on DTA curve stands out around 100 °C, which may be associated with the evaporation of moisture from the particle, but may also be related to the glass transition of this material, due to its amorphous characteristic [22].

Regarding to the added crosslinkers, it is observed that the DTA curves of all formulations are very similar, but with different intensities. In this case, it can be concluded that the four crosslinking agents can be incorporated, evidencing their compatibility with the polymer blend and the drug.

The Hot-Stage analytical technique (HSM) was performed for the most satisfactory formulations (VPRO2 and V2PRO2) also in order to visualize the thermal processes and the results are displayed in Fig. 10 .

In the VPRO2 and V2PRO2 formulations, the drug is visible in the particles up to a temperature of approximately 110 °C. At a temperature of 150 °C, its complete melting process can already be seen. After 200 °C, particles composed of sericin, alginate, valsartan and proanthocyanidin start to decompose, with bubbles formation on the surface. Comparing this result with the Hot-Stage analysis for the formulation without crosslinking agent, in which the particles present complete drug melting at 130 °C, it can be concluded that the incorporation of proanthocyanidin in the formulation slightly improves the thermal stability of the drug. Furthermore, the increase in alginate content did not seem to affect this characteristic.

The functional groups present in the particles were evaluated by spectroscopy in the infrared region and the results can be observed in Fig. 11 . This technique was applied only in formulations with different types of crosslinking agents and also to the best formulations obtained (VPRO2 and V2PRO2).

Comparing the spectrum obtained by the pure drug (VAL) and the formulations containing valsartan (VAC2, VPVA2, VPEG2, VPRO2 and V2PRO2), peaks and characteristic bands of this drug are visualized and not present in SAPRO2 and SA2PRO2 formulations, both drug-free. According to Ardiana et al. [54], specific peaks of valsartan around 3035 cm−1 are reported for the O−H stretch, other bands positioned at 2873 and 2927 cm−1 are attributed to the C−H stretch. At 1734 and 1602 cm−1, the presence of C=O stretches is verified, representing the acid and amine group. The bands of 1473 and 1448 cm−1 are attributed to the curvature of the C-OH plane. In addition, bands around 1390 and 995 cm−1 indicate the C−O and C−N stretches, respectively. The presence of these peaks and bands in the formulations produced in the present work confirms the incorporation of the drug into the blend of sericin and alginate and its compatibility with the matrix components.

PEG has a characteristic spectrum of low intensity peaks between 1456 and 1353 cm−1 due to vibrations caused by C−H deformation and between 1200 and 1100 cm−1 because of the vibrations of the ether group C-O-C stretching [55]. As for PVA, characteristic peaks of the material are present between 1730 and 1680 cm−1 and are attributed to the stretches of C=O and C−O, results of the remaining acetate groups by the saponification reaction of polyvinyl acetate [56,57]. Typical FTIR spectra of citric acid can also be seen by Fig. 11, such as peaks related to the stretching of the O−H bond around 3300 cm−1, stretching related to C=O bond in approximately 1720 cm−1 and elongation of the C-OH bond around 1200 cm−1 [58,59].

In the spectrum for formulations containing the proanthocyanidin crosslinking agent, the absorption band referring to the peak at approximately 3365 cm−1 was attributed to the formation of the hydrogen bond between the phenolic hydroxyls of the proanthocyanidins. The absorption bands at 1610 and 1105 cm−1 are generally given to the functional groups characteristic of polyflavonoids, while the bands of approximately 1520 and 777 cm−1 refers to the elongation of the aromatic ring and the deformation of the C−H bond [60,61].

In relation to the different structures of sericin, bands in 1655, 1630 and 1645 cm−1 refers to the α-helix, β-sheet and random coil structures [62]. As for alginate, characteristic bands in the range 1000–1200 cm- 1 indicates the C-O characteristic absorption of saccharides and in 1427 cm- 1 related to the C=O elongation of the carboxylic acid salts [63].

Fig. 12 displays the diffractogram of the pure drug, which did not present well-defined crystallographic peaks, confirming that the structure of valsartan used in the present study is amorphous. The profiles presented for both more promising formulations VPRO2 and V2PRO2 show wide peaks between 20 and 25° in a similar way to the one of the pure drug (VAL), not noted in the SAPRO2 and SA2PRO2 formulations, but with a broader aspect, due to the presence of proanthocyanidin and sericin, both also of amorphous character [22]. Despite the amorphous characteristic, sericin shows low intensity peaks at 19°, due to the crystalline structure of β-sheet [64] and alginate shows peaks at 13°, 21° and 45° referring to the polygururonate, polymanuronate and other amorphous unit [20,65].

Accelerated stability study was conducted from a quantity of particles relative to 160 mg stored by pharmaceutical capsules of 0 (zero) size and deposited in blisters. The particles used in this test were chosen covering the best formulations. The medication blister packs were kept in a climatic chamber with a controlled temperature of 40 ± 2 °C and a relative humidity of 75 ± 5% for 6 months, following the parameters established by the FDA (Food and Drug Administration, USA, 2003), Guidance for Industry Q1A(R2) Stability Testing of New Drug Substances and Products [66].

After 90 days (3 months) elapsed, the IE and DL were measured to assess whether there was loss of drug by the polymeric matrix. In addition, the dissolution test was also conducted to analyze possible changes in the release profile of the API. At 180 days (6 months), the same tests were performed on the proposed formulations. Table 5 presents the results obtained for IE and DL after 3 and 6 months of testing, in comparison with the particles that were not subjected to forced conditions of temperature and humidity.

With the Tukey test performed, it appears that the IE and DL by the VPRO2 formulation was similar after 3 and 6 months of testing, indicating good drug stability in this formulation. The formulation V2PRO2, on the other hand, exhibited a 95% confidence significant difference for IE result after 3 and 6 months, both with performance decay. The drug's carrying capacity for this formulation was decreased after the first 90 days, but remained statistically similar until the end of the stipulated study period.

The VPRO2 formulation, both after 3 months and after 6 months of testing, presented a difference factor between 0 and 15 and a similarity factor between 50 and 100 (Table 6). This formulation managed to maintain the favorable release profile, in addition to maintaining IE and DL satisfactory. The V2PRO2 formulation displayed a slightly different character in the same period, evidencing that valsartan, in this formulation, has undergone more changes.

In addition, proanthocyanidin improved the visual appearance of hard gelatin capsules after the period at the climate chamber, decreasing the adhesion between the particles and the capsule, probably caused by the remaining moisture in the particles.

The incorporation of valsartan in the blend of sericin and alginate, together with different types of covalent crosslinking agents, occurred satisfactorily and presented results compatible with other drugs already tested in this same polymeric matrix. In general, the addition of a crosslinker favored the increase in the efficiency of incorporation and loading of the drug by the particles, with the proanthocyanidin at 2% (w/V) being more promising and suitable for the modified release of valsartan, in addition to being a natural substance extracted mainly from grape seed extract. Citric acid and PVA provided higher deviation in the particles size, caused by the less favorable formation by the physical structure of these particles. The effective incorporation and the compatibility with the polymer blend of valsartan was confirmed by the SEM, XRD and FTIR analytical techniques. Thermal behavior of the drug and its fusion and decomposition processes were evidenced by DTG/TGA and HSM, with the addition of proanthocyanidin slightly improving the thermal stability in relation to the temperature increase. With the accelerated stability test, the addition of proanthocyanidin considerably minimized the interaction between the particle moisture and the hard gelatinous capsule. In addition, the formulations maintained satisfactory results of incorporation efficiency, drug loading and in vitro drug release after 3 and 6 months of conditioning at forced temperature and relative humidity, proving the stability of valsartan on this modified pharmaceutical form.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Council for Scientific and Technological Development, CNPq (Grant number 301401/2016-0), The São Paulo Research Foundation, FAPESP (Grant numbers 2015/13505-9, 2016/05007-1) and Coordination for the Improvement of Higher Education Personnel, CAPES (Grant number 88882.329675/2019-01). The authors would like to thank the BRATAC Silk Mills Company for providing the silkworm cocoons.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gastro-resistant characteristics of GRAS-grade enteric coatings for pharmaceutical and nutraceutical products
